name: Personality Congruence and Interpersonal Attraction
core_claim: This is not a measurement instrument but a body of social-psychology theory about WHEN and WHETHER similarity between two people (in personality, attitudes, and values) drives mutual attraction and dyadic satisfaction. Two competing hypotheses are in play: the similarity-attraction effect (Byrne's classic 'birds of a feather' / consensual-validation theory — we like people who match us because they validate our worldview) versus the complementarity hypothesis ('opposites attract' — partners satisfy each other's needs through difference, e.g. dominant-submissive pairings). For HelpME2C the construct matters because the product's core bet is that a personality/taste profile of two people can be combined to predict what they will JOINTLY enjoy watching. The theoretical link to joint media preference is: IF congruent personalities reliably converge on the same content, a two-person recommender can blend profiles confidently and lean on overlap; IF complementarity or value-divergence dominates, blending profiles by similarity is the wrong model and the system must instead negotiate difference (intersection, least-misery, turn-taking). The headline empirical verdict the literature supports: actual personality-trait similarity is a WEAK predictor of relationship satisfaction once you control for each person's own traits; shared VALUES/attitudes and PERCEIVED similarity matter more than shared Big Five traits; and the dimension that most consistently predicts dyadic satisfaction is not similarity at all but trait POSITIVITY (both partners being low-neuroticism / high-agreeableness). For a recommender this reframes the task: congruence is a useful weak prior, not a load-bearing mechanism.
predictive_validity_for_media: There is almost NO direct evidence linking personality congruence between two people to JOINT media enjoyment — the construct's validity is established for attraction and relationship satisfaction, and must be extrapolated (with caution) to co-viewing. The relevant evidence chain: (1) Similarity-attraction effect is large but FRAGILE across relationship stage. Montoya, Horton & Kirchner (2008) meta-analysis (460 effect sizes, 313 studies) found actual similarity correlated with attraction r=0.47 and perceived similarity r=0.39 OVERALL — but actual similarity's effect was significant only in no-interaction / short-interaction (zero-acquaintance) studies and was NON-SIGNIFICANT in existing relationships, whereas perceived similarity predicted attraction at every stage. Implication: 'matching personalities' predicts initial spark in a lab but does NOT predict satisfaction in established couples. (2) Actual personality-trait similarity weakly predicts relationship satisfaction. Multiple studies controlling for main effects find similarity effects near zero or very small; the often-cited large trait-satisfaction correlations (e.g. r around 0.78-0.90 for openness/agreeableness/conscientiousness in one meta-analysis) are ACTOR effects of one's own/partner's traits, NOT similarity effects, and are outliers that should be treated skeptically. (3) The best dyadic finding (Weidmann et al. / Frontiers 2018, 5 studies, ~34-91 dyads each) shows that profile DISTANCE (dissimilarity) and profile POSITIVITY act as reciprocal suppressors: distance combined with positivity is a consistently strong predictor (distance betas around -0.5 to -0.77 in couples), but positivity is doing much of the work — low-distance-AND-positive beats raw similarity. (4) Values/attitude similarity outperforms trait similarity (Gonzaga; Leikas et al. 2018) but effects are modest and sometimes gender-specific. NO located study measures effect sizes for personality congruence -> joint TV/film/anime preference or co-viewing satisfaction specifically. Net: congruence predicts attraction strongly at zero-acquaintance (relevant to a cold matching context) but weakly predicts sustained joint satisfaction; shared values > shared traits; positivity > similarity. [uncertain]
instrument_design: Congruence is computed, not asked — there is no questionnaire for 'congruence' itself; it is a DERIVED dyadic metric over two individually-measured profiles. So the instrument question reduces to (a) which per-person instrument feeds it and (b) which dyadic aggregation function is applied. Per-person input is typically a Big Five short form (BFI-44, BFI-10, TIPI) or a values measure (Schwartz PVQ). Aggregation functions used in the literature: profile correlation across items (intraclass / Q-correlation), mean absolute difference across items ('distance' — the Frontiers 2018 method averaged absolute differences across 32 items), and Euclidean distance in trait space. For a sub-2-minute onboarding, the realistic build is: collect ~6-10 items per person (one Big-Five-style item per dimension plus 1-2 values items), then compute a per-dimension absolute-difference vector between the two partners. The minimum viable congruence signal is a per-dimension gap on the 2-3 dimensions that plausibly matter for content (Openness gap for complexity tolerance; a values gap for moral-tone tolerance) plus a coarse aggregate distance. Note this REQUIRES both partners to complete onboarding — congruence is undefined for a single user, which is a hard product constraint. [uncertain] (item counts and which gaps to weight are engineering judgements, not a validated short form.)
cold_start_applicability: High relevance to a TWO-PERSON cold-start specifically, with an important caveat. The strongest part of the congruence evidence is the zero-acquaintance / no-interaction case — exactly the cold-start condition where two users have just been paired and there is no joint viewing history. At zero acquaintance, actual similarity has its LARGEST effect on attraction (Montoya et al. 2008), so a similarity prior is at its most defensible precisely when the system has nothing else. This means a congruence score can serve as a genuine first-pass prior on 'how easily will these two converge' before any co-viewing data exists, and it needs zero viewing history (only two short personality/values profiles). Cross-medium inference is inherited from whatever per-person instrument is used (e.g. Big Five Openness transfers a complexity-tolerance prior across TV/anime). The caveat: the cold-start prior decays in exactly the direction the product wants — as the pair actually watches together, perceived similarity and accumulated joint signal should dominate and the actual-similarity prior should be down-weighted. So congruence is a launch prior with a short half-life, not a durable model. Signal quality before first interaction: moderate-to-good as a soft convergence prior; weak as a satisfaction predictor. [uncertain]
group_compatibility_use: This construct IS the two-person use case — it is the most directly relevant entry in this research set, because congruence is inherently dyadic. Combination strategies and what the evidence says: (1) AVERAGING / midpoint blending — defensible when the pair is congruent (low distance), because both profiles point the same way; the literature's similarity-attraction signal supports leaning on overlap when distance is low. (2) INTERSECTION / AND — recommend only where both individually-predicted preferences agree; appropriate when distance is moderate, and aligns with the finding that perceived similarity (felt agreement) sustains satisfaction. (3) LEAST-MISERY — protect the more sensitive partner on aversive content; this is the correct strategy where the pair DIVERGES on a dimension (high distance on Emotionality/neuroticism -> veto graphic existential-horror for the high-Emotionality partner). The single most important evidence-based steer: do NOT model joint satisfaction as raw trait similarity. The Frontiers 2018 dyadic work shows POSITIVITY (both partners low-neuroticism / agreeable) combined with low distance predicts satisfaction far better than similarity alone — so a joint recommender should up-weight content that suits a positive-disposition blend and treat large distance as a flag to switch from averaging to least-misery, rather than rewarding similarity per se. Also: shared VALUES predict joint satisfaction better than shared traits, so a values-overlap term should carry more weight in the blend than a Big-Five-overlap term. NO located study validates which aggregation strategy maximises JOINT VIEWING satisfaction specifically — this is reasoned from attraction/satisfaction dyadic data, not a measured co-viewing result. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: The per-person inputs (Big Five short form, values items) carry the usual self-report problems: acquiescence bias (mitigate with reverse-keyed items), social-desirability pull (mild in a low-stakes entertainment context), and faking. The congruence-specific UX wrinkle is PERCEIVED vs ACTUAL similarity: because perceived similarity is the stronger sustained predictor, there is a temptation to ASK each partner how similar they think they are — but self-rated perceived similarity is heavily biased (people in good relationships over-report similarity, a halo/assumed-similarity effect), so directly eliciting 'how alike are you?' yields a satisfaction-contaminated, near-circular signal. Better to compute actual congruence from independent per-person answers and avoid asking either partner to rate the other or rate their similarity. A second wrinkle: because both partners must answer independently and HONESTLY for the gap to be meaningful, the quiz must be completed separately (not co-located answering, which produces convergent-faking / acquiescence-to-partner). Recommended format: short forced-choice or this-or-that per-person items, completed privately, with congruence computed server-side and never shown back as a raw 'compatibility score' (which would create expectancy effects and is privacy-fraught — see below). [uncertain]
cross_cultural_validity: The similarity-attraction effect is one of the more cross-culturally robust findings in social psychology and replicates broadly, but with two material qualifications for HelpME2C's multilingual reach. (1) The RELATIVE weight of similarity vs other forces varies by culture: collectivist East-Asian contexts emphasise relational harmony and family/contextual fit, and some research suggests the similarity-attraction link, while present, is moderated by cultural self-construal (interdependent vs independent). Values-similarity effects in particular are culturally loaded because the VALUE CONTENT differs across cultures (Schwartz values have cross-cultural structure but mean levels and salience differ). (2) The per-person instruments inherit their own cross-cultural limits — Big Five short forms have decent but imperfect invariance across languages; values measures (Schwartz PVQ) are explicitly cross-culturally validated and may be the safer multilingual input. The congruence COMPUTATION (a difference function) is culture-neutral mechanically, but what counts as a 'large' or satisfaction-relevant gap is not, so any threshold on distance should not be assumed constant across HelpME2C's Korean/Japanese/French/German/Spanish/Italian/Chinese/Portuguese/Hindi/Arabic user base. Coverage of the similarity-attraction moderation literature for Hindi/Arabic-speaking populations specifically was not located here and should be treated as unverified. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Compounded sensitivity beyond single-person profiling. The per-person personality/values data already edges toward GDPR Article 9 special-category territory (personality can imply mental health via neuroticism, and values can imply religious/political belief). The DYADIC product adds two distinct risks: (1) a computed 'compatibility'/congruence score is a sensitive inference ABOUT A RELATIONSHIP between two identifiable people, and exposing it (e.g. 'you are 62% compatible') could be relationally harmful and is a sensitive inference neither party may have consented to seeing about the other; (2) one partner's profile is, in effect, processed against the other's — each user must consent not just to their own profiling but to it being combined with their partner's. Recommend: explicit, granular, per-user consent for personality/values collection AND for dyadic combination; compute congruence transiently to bias recommendations and prefer NOT to persist a raw compatibility score; never surface a numeric compatibility/morality score back to either user. This intersects ADR-0012 (deletion/anonymisation) and the CLAUDE.md §4 stop-and-ask on privacy-control flows — and note the deletion edge case: if one partner exercises erasure, the other's derived joint signal must be unwound too. [uncertain] (exact Article 9 classification of inferred personality and of a derived dyadic score is legally unsettled; treat conservatively.)
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Congruence does not map to themes directly the way a trait does; it maps a DYADIC GAP onto a recommendation STRATEGY per theme cluster. Mechanism: take each partner's per-dimension theme predisposition (from the individual instrument) and let the gap choose averaging vs intersection vs least-misery. Concrete mappings: LOW Openness-gap (both similarly high) -> confidently blend toward demanding texture themes: nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity, slow-burn, character-study, magical-realism, meditation-on-time, cosmic-horror. HIGH Openness-gap (one craves complexity, one wants accessibility) -> intersection toward middlebrow crowd-pleasers and away from polarising art-house; favour mystery-investigation, whodunit, heist-and-caper, underdog-rise as safe common ground. HIGH Emotionality/neuroticism-gap -> least-misery on aversive content: veto or down-weight existential-horror, folk-horror, survival-and-endurance, trauma-survival, war-and-its-toll for the more sensitive partner; steer to found-family, friendship-tested, fish-out-of-water, absurdist-comedy. LOW VALUES-gap (shared moral worldview — the higher-weight term) -> confidently serve morally-charged content matched to the shared value direction: redemption-arc, guilt-and-atonement, ethical-dilemma, betrayal-and-forgiveness, or conversely power-corrupts / moral-compromise if both lean transgressive. HIGH VALUES-gap -> avoid morally-divisive themes (complicity-with-evil, vigilante-justice, propaganda-and-truth, satire-of-power) where the pair would diverge on whether the protagonist is admirable; route to value-neutral common ground (ensemble-mosaic, workplace-procedural, fish-out-of-water, chase-and-escape). High POSITIVITY blend (both agreeable/low-neuroticism) -> tilt the whole slate warmer: found-family, platonic-soulmates, coming-of-age, redemption-arc over relentless toxic-relationship / loneliness-and-isolation. These mappings are reasoned extrapolations, not validated against joint-viewing data. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: PARTIAL include — adopt congruence as a dyadic STRATEGY SELECTOR, not as a standalone satisfaction predictor or a user-facing compatibility score. Rationale tied to the North Star (helping two people discover shows they both want to watch): congruence is the most directly on-point construct in this research set because it is inherently two-person and is strongest exactly at cold-start. Recommended use: (1) compute a per-dimension distance vector and a values-overlap term from each partner's short onboarding profile (do NOT build a separate congruence quiz); (2) use the gaps to choose aggregation per theme cluster — low gap -> average/blend, moderate gap -> intersection, high gap on aversive/moral dimensions -> least-misery (per group_compatibility_use); (3) WEIGHT shared values above shared traits, and incorporate a positivity term (both low-neuroticism/agreeable) which the evidence shows out-predicts similarity; (4) treat the actual-similarity prior as a launch-only signal with a short half-life — down-weight it as real co-viewing data accrues, since actual similarity stops predicting satisfaction in established pairs. Do NOT model joint satisfaction as raw trait similarity, do NOT expose a compatibility number, and do NOT ask either partner to rate their similarity (assumed-similarity bias). Hard dependencies: (a) requires BOTH partners to complete onboarding (congruence is undefined solo), so it cannot serve the single-user cold-start; (b) only useful once a per-person instrument (Big Five short form and/or a values elicitation) is in place — it is a layer on top of those, not a substitute; (c) the values-overlap term, which carries the most weight, depends on having any values elicitation at all. Before relying on congruence-driven strategy switching in production, validate against actual joint-viewing outcomes — the strategy mapping is currently theory. [uncertain]
key_sources: Montoya, Horton & Kirchner (2008) 'Is actual similarity necessary for attraction? A meta-analysis of actual and perceived similarity' Journal of Social and Personal Relationships 25(6):889-922 — the key meta-analysis (SAGE, paywalled; PDF via ResearchGate, free). Montoya & Horton (2013) 'A meta-analytic investigation of the processes underlying the similarity-attraction effect' JSPR (SAGE, paywalled; ResearchGate PDF). Weidmann et al. (2018) 'Similarity and Positivity of Personality Profiles Consistently Predict Relationship Satisfaction in Dyads' Frontiers in Psychology 9:1009 / PMC6034067 (FREE) — the distance-vs-positivity suppressor finding. Leikas, Ilmarinen et al. (2018) 'Relationship satisfaction and similarity of personality traits, personal values, and attitudes' Personality and Individual Differences (ScienceDirect, paywalled; ScienceDirect S0191886917306815) — values > traits. Furler, Gomez & Grob (2014) 'Personality similarity and life satisfaction in couples' / two-wave panel study on similarity of life goals, values and personality (ResearchGate). Dyrenforth et al. (large nationally representative samples — actor/partner effects dominate similarity). Byrne (1971) 'The Attraction Paradigm' — foundational similarity-attraction theory (book, paywalled). Cross-cultural overview and Psychology Today 'Does Similarity Lead to Attraction and Compatibility?' (free, popular summary). Co-viewing context (sparse): pilot fNIRS hyperscanning study, PMC12196001 (free) — co-viewing emotive video and relationship-satisfaction mismatch; not a preference study.
